question_text: Why does a person sink deeper into soft snow when standing still compared to when they are moving quickly across it?

options.label: A
options.text: The snow is colder when they stand still

options.label: B
options.text: Standing still concentrates their weight over a smaller contact area for longer, creating greater pressure and deeper sinking

options.label: C
options.text: Moving quickly compresses the snow more effectively

options.label: D
options.text: The person's weight increases when they stand still

correct_answer: Standing still concentrates their weight over a smaller contact area for longer, creating greater pressure and deeper sinking
explanation: Pressure is calculated as force divided by area (P = F/A). When a person stands still, their weight (force) acts on the small area of their feet for a long time, creating high pressure that causes the snow to deform and compress beneath them. When moving quickly, the force is distributed over a larger area as they move, and the contact time is shorter, resulting in lower pressure and less sinking.
